label: 221_trade finance_letters credit_lcs_trade credit
label_human: Trade finance services for international commerce
description: Trade finance involves various financial instruments like letters of credit that banks provide to reduce risks in international trade. These services are crucial but their exact importance remains debated, with recent studies focusing on U.S. bank activities and the role of LCs.

earliest_articles.title: Export credit agencies to the rescue of trade finance
earliest_articles.excerpt: New data on export insurance and guarantees suggest that export credit agencies (ECAs) have played an important role in preventing a complete drying up of trade finance markets during the current financial crisis. These efforts have complemented the support provided by international financial institutions and regional…

earliest_articles.title: Off the cliff and back? Credit conditions and international trade during the global financial crisis
earliest_articles.excerpt: The global financial crisis has decimated international trade. What started out as a credit crisis in the US financial sector quickly spilled over into a broader contraction in industrial production and trade for many countries. In all, world trade is expected to have fallen by up to 9% in 2009 (WTO 2009). These…

earliest_articles.title: The prudential treatment of trade finance under Basel III: For a fair treatment
earliest_articles.excerpt: There was a time when trade finance received favourable regulatory treatment. It was viewed as one of the safest, most collateralised, and self-liquidating forms of finance. This was reflected in the moderate of capitalisation for cross-border trade credit in the form of letters of credit and similar securitised…

newest_articles.title: Trade insurance matters: Evidence from the Covid-19 pandemic
newest_articles.excerpt: Cross-border trade is risky. Trade partners have to decide who bears the risks associated with each transaction, be it financial, commercial, political, or adverse business risk. But risks can be mitigated. One way of doing so is to use a trade-financed instrument called a ‘letter of credit’ (LC). In an LC-financed…

central_articles.title: Trade finance around the world
central_articles.excerpt: Financial institutions play an important role in international commerce. They provide financing to importers and exporters, offer trade credit insurance and factoring services, and sell specific trade finance products. But just how important these banking services are for international trade remains an open question,…

central_articles.title: The role of bank guarantees in international trade
central_articles.excerpt: International trade is a risky activity – importers may not pay after receiving the goods and exporters may not deliver if they are paid in advance. To reduce the risk of international commerce, banks offer specific trade finance products, the most prominent being letters of credit (LCs). In recent years, policymakers…
